street.thesis: Netflix is a durable content platform transitioning into a two-sided advertising business; the bull case rests on advertising reaching $3B in 2026 and scaling further, combined with operating leverage from the ~330M subscriber base driving earnings faster than revenue. (Source: market_facts.json street_thesis_one_line)
street.rating: Moderate Buy (29 Strong Buy, 8 Buy, 13 Hold, 0 Sell per market_facts.json)

history_context.note: Netflix traded at 30-40x EV/NOPAT during the 2020-2021 growth peak (pre-correction), fell to 15-20x during the 2022 crash when subscriber growth stalled, and recovered to 25-35x in 2023-2025 as the password-crackdown drove subscriber reacceleration. The current 26x (EV $336B / NTM NOPAT $12.9B) is at the low end of the post-correction trading range, reflecting the Q1 2026 'beat-but-no-raise' disappointment and BofA downgrade. Historical context: Netflix at $78 is -42% from the $134 June 2025 all-time high (post-split basis). Source: market_facts.json, databundle.json.

duality_decomposition.note: single-stage justified forward P/E = (1 - g/ROIC)/(r - g); shows the ROIC/payout lever

peers.name: YouTube (Alphabet segment)
peers.note: YouTube advertising revenue ~$35B in 2025; estimated NOPAT with 25% margin = $8.75B; EV implied for the YouTube business at ~$220B (from Alphabet sum-of-parts) = ~25x EV/NOPAT. YouTube is the primary direct competitor for streaming video advertising budgets (12.7% US TV viewing share per Nielsen Dec 2025 vs. Netflix 9.0%).

peers.name: Walt Disney (DIS) -- streaming assets
peers.note: Disney+ + Hulu combined streaming NOPAT approaching profitability; Disney overall EV/EBITDA ~11x, but the streaming segment deserves a premium. Estimated Disney streaming EV/NOPAT in the 15-20x range as it scales from breakeven. Disney is a secondary competitor; combined Disney+/Hulu at ~180M US subscribers post-merger is a meaningful UCAN competitive threat.

peers.name: Amazon Prime Video (AWS subsidy context)
peers.note: Prime Video is bundled with Prime membership; not separately monetized. Amazon's advertising revenue (including Prime Video ads) was ~$56B in 2024, but is not directly comparable to Netflix's streaming-only advertising. Amazon is a meaningful streaming competitor but the multiple is not isolatable.

peers.name: Comcast (CMCSA) -- NBCUniversal Peacock
peers.note: Comcast at ~10x EV/EBITDA; Peacock is a sub-scale streaming service at $4B+ revenue. Legacy cable business drags the multiple down. Reference for mature cable media valuations.

peers.name: Spotify (SPOT) -- subscription analogy
peers.note: Spotify at ~38x EV/NOPAT as a profitable subscription audio platform growing at ~20%. Relevant as a subscription-plus-advertising model precedent, though Spotify's content cost structure differs materially (music licensing is more like a royalty than Netflix's content amortization).

reconstruction.what_price_implies: At 25.4x NTM NOPAT (FY2026E), the current price implies a ~9.7% earnings CAGR for 10y (then 4% terminal) at r=10.3%.
reconstruction.what_street_implies: The street mean target implies ~14.8% for 10y.
reconstruction.history_anchor: Netflix traded at 30-40x EV/NOPAT during the 2020-2021 growth peak (pre-correction), fell to 15-20x during the 2022 crash when subscriber growth stalled, and recovered to 25-35x in 2023-2025 as the password-crackdown drove subscriber reacceleration. The current 26x (EV $336B / NTM NOPAT $12.9B) is at the low end of the post-correction trading range, reflecting the Q1 2026 'beat-but-no-raise' disappointment and BofA downgrade. Historical context: Netflix at $78 is -42% from the $134 June 2025 all-time high (post-split basis). Source: market_facts.json, databundle.json.
reconstruction.contested_lever: Advertising revenue trajectory 2027-2030 (the crux per Theia): bears argue YouTube's 12.7% vs. Netflix's 9.0% US viewing share makes it structurally disadvantaged for advertiser budgets; bulls argue Netflix's premium, appointment-viewing environment commands premium CPMs ($40-65) that make share of viewership less relevant than CPM quality. This is the lever that differentiates base ($7.2B by 2030) from bull ($10B) from bear ($4B).
